keyPoints: Index Laws: aᵐ × aⁿ = aᵐ⁺ⁿ; aᵐ ÷ aⁿ = aᵐ⁻ⁿ; (aᵐ)ⁿ = aᵐⁿ
Fractional Indices: a to the power of 1/m is the m-th root of a
Negative Indices: a⁻ᵐ = 1 ÷ aᵐ
Difference of Two Squares: x² - y² = (x + y)(x - y)
Surd Multiplication: √(ab) = √a × √b
Surd Division: √(a ÷ b) = √a ÷ √b
Rationalising Denominators: Multiply numerator and denominator by a - √b for fractions containing a + √b
Factorising Quadratics: Find factors of ac that sum to b to factorise ax² + bx + c
Quadratic Equation Form: ax² + bx + c = 0 where a ≠ 0
Quadratic Formula: x = (-b ± √(b² - 4ac)) ÷ 2a
Completing the Square: x² + bx = (x + b/2)² - (b/2)²
General Completed Square: ax² + bx + c = a(x + b/2a)² + (c - b²/4a)
Turning Point: The minimum or maximum point of a quadratic graph y = a(x + p)² + q is at (-p, q)
Discriminant: b² - 4ac determines the number of real roots of a quadratic equation
Two Distinct Real Roots: b² - 4ac > 0
One Repeated Root: b² - 4ac = 0
No Real Roots: b² - 4ac < 0
Linear Simultaneous Equations: Can be solved using the methods of elimination or substitution
Quadratic Simultaneous Equations: Require substitution from the linear equation to the quadratic equation
Graphical Intersections: Solutions to simultaneous equations represent the coordinates where their graphs intersect
Linear Inequalities: Solved similarly to equations but the inequality sign reverses when multiplying or dividing by a negative number
Quadratic Inequalities: Rearrange to f(x) > 0 or f(x) < 0, find critical values, and sketch the graph to deduce the solution set
Curve Positions: The inequality f(x) < g(x) represents the x-values where the curve y = f(x) is below the curve y = g(x)
Regions: The inequality y < f(x) represents the coordinate area strictly below the curve y = f(x)
Solid vs Dotted Lines: Use a solid line for ≤ or ≥ and a dotted line for < or > when defining regions on a graph
Cubic Graphs: f(x) = ax³ + bx² + cx + d has up to 3 roots and crosses the y-axis at the constant d
Cubic Roots: If p is a root of f(x), then the graph of y = f(x) touches or crosses the x-axis at (p, 0)
Reciprocal Graphs: y = a ÷ x has asymptotes at x = 0 and y = 0
Inverse Square Graphs: y = a ÷ x² has asymptotes at x = 0 and y = 0 and the y-values are always positive if a > 0
Vertical Translation: The transformation y = f(x) + a shifts the graph vertically by a units
Horizontal Translation: The transformation y = f(x + a) shifts the graph horizontally by -a units
Vertical Stretch: The transformation y = af(x) stretches the graph vertically by a scale factor of a
Horizontal Stretch: The transformation y = f(ax) stretches the graph horizontally by a scale factor of 1/a
Equation of a Line: y = mx + c where m is the gradient and c is the y-intercept
Gradient Formula: m = (y₂ - y₁) ÷ (x₂ - x₁)
Point-Gradient Equation: y - y₁ = m(x - x₁)
General Form of a Line: ax + by + c = 0 where a, b, and c are integers
Parallel Lines: Have identical gradients where m₁ = m₂
Perpendicular Lines: Gradients multiply to -1 where m₁ × m₂ = -1
Distance Between Points: d = √((x₂ - x₁)² + (y₂ - y₁)²)
Midpoint Formula: M = ((x₁ + x₂) ÷ 2, (y₁ + y₂) ÷ 2)
Cosine Rule (Side Length): a² = b² + c² - 2bc cos(A)
Cosine Rule (Angle): cos(A) = (b² + c² - a²) ÷ 2bc
Sine Rule: a ÷ sin(A) = b ÷ sin(B) = c ÷ sin(C)
Area of a Triangle: Area = ½ab sin(C)
Sine Graph: Period is 360°, ranges from -1 to 1, and passes through the origin
Cosine Graph: Period is 360°, ranges from -1 to 1, and the y-intercept is 1
Tangent Graph: Period is 180° with asymptotes at x = 90° ± 180°n
Transforming Trigonometric Graphs: y = sin(x + θ) shifts the sine graph horizontally by -θ
Radian Measure: 180° = π radians
Radian Conversion: To convert degrees to radians, multiply by π ÷ 180
Arc Length Formula: s = rθ where θ is measured in radians
Sector Area Formula: A = ½r²θ where θ is measured in radians
Segment Area: Area of a segment = ½r²(θ - sin(θ)) where θ is in radians
Perimeter of a Sector: Perimeter = 2r + rθ where θ is in radians
Area of a Triangle in Radians: Area = ½ab sin(C) applies directly when C is in radians
Exact Trigonometric Values: Familiarity with exact radian values such as sin(π ÷ 6) = 1 ÷ 2 is required
Gradient of a Curve: The gradient of a curve at a point is the gradient of the tangent to the curve at that point
Power Rule: If y = xⁿ, then dy ÷ dx = nxⁿ⁻¹
Constant Rule: The derivative of any constant value is 0
Multiple Terms: Differentiate each term independently for expressions involving addition or subtraction
Finding Gradients: Substitute the given x-value into the derivative f'(x) to evaluate the gradient at a specific point
Equation of Tangent: y - y₁ = m(x - x₁) where the gradient m = f'(x₁)
Equation of Normal: y - y₁ = (-1 ÷ m)(x - x₁) where the normal gradient is the negative reciprocal of f'(x₁)
Second Order Derivatives: f''(x) or d²y ÷ dx² is obtained by differentiating the first derivative
Indefinite Integration: Integration is the reverse of differentiation; ∫ f'(x) dx = f(x) + c
Power Rule for Integration: ∫xⁿ dx = (xⁿ⁺¹ ÷ (n + 1)) + c, valid for all n ≠ -1
Constant of Integration: Always include the constant + c for all indefinite integrals
Multiple Terms: Integrate each term in a polynomial independently
Constant Multiples: ∫kf(x) dx = k∫f(x) dx where k is a constant multiplier
Finding the Function: If the derivative f'(x) is known, the original function is f(x) = ∫f'(x) dx
Evaluating c: Substitute a given coordinate (x, y) on the curve into the integrated equation to solve for the constant of integration
Fractional Powers: Convert all roots and reciprocal terms to fractional or negative indices before integrating
